name: ai_music_training_opt_out_label_licensing_deadlock
premise: Streaming general counsel Casey Reyes must decide whether to pull a generative music feature after major labels demand retroactive training opt-outs that would degrade personalization for indie artists who consented. Artist advocate Tara Garcia argues indie discovery collapses if catalogs fragment. Keeping the feature risks contempt if courts side with labels in a preliminary injunction filed for Friday. Turning it off angers subscribers who paid annual plans. External scrutiny from regulators and community observers has intensified because the outcome could redefine expectations in Intellectual Property. The dilemma is widely framed as a rights vs utility tradeoff under incomplete information and tightening deadlines that discourage indefinite delay.

agents.name: Casey Reyes
agents.identity: I am the streaming general counsel balancing copyright exposure, subscriber contracts, and fairness to long-tail artists who depend on algorithmic discovery.
agents.goal: I want label compliance without erasing indie consent frameworks we marketed as ethical differentiators.

agents.name: Tara Garcia
agents.identity: I am an artist advocate who negotiated indie consent templates and who fears fragmentation will bury non-label music overnight.
agents.goal: I want the feature frozen until tri-party licensing creates a pooled training corpus with transparent splits, even if that takes months.

choices: I will pull the generative feature immediately and issue prorated refunds for annual subscribers, accepting churn to competitors with riskier models.
I will keep the feature for indie-consented-only corpora and geo-block label-heavy markets pending injunction outcome, accepting uneven product experience and partial contempt exposure.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Judge set a conference call at four p.m. Casey Reyes must choose between pulling the generative feature with refunds or keeping a consent-filtered subset with geo-blocks. What does Casey Reyes do?
